description: The provided image is unreadable and displays only a low-resolution interface icon, so no scientific content, axes, curves, or microstructural features can be visually confirmed. Based on the surrounding paper context for Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steels, the intended figure likely reports mechanical property data or microstructural evidence for the 4Al, 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al alloys. The text summary references engineering and true stress-strain curves, strain-hardening rate plots, microhardness versus strain data, and post-deformation TEM images. For the ferrite-austenite duplex 8Al steel, the caption states a yield strength of 455 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 740 MPa, and total elongation of 39.8%, linked to dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite. In contrast, the 10Al steel exhibits brittle fracture with merely 4.6% elongation due to limited screw dislocation activity. Should this figure depict the stress-strain behavior, it would contrast the gradual strength increase and ductility loss across the Al series, highlighting the optimal strength-ductility balance in the 8Al duplex microstructure. All specific figure details, however, remain unverified from the actual image.

This figure presents a comprehensive microstructural characterization of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x = 4, 6, 8, 10 wt.%) low-density steels after solution treatment at 1050°C for 2 h and oil quenching. Panels (a–d) are EBSD phase maps showing the transition from single-phase austenite (γ, red) in 4Al steel to austenite-based duplex in 6Al, ferrite-based (α, blue) duplex in 8Al, and nearly full ferrite in 10Al. Panels (e–h) display [001]γ selected area diffraction patterns revealing satellite spots from spinodal decomposition of Al and C, plus L1₂ short-range order whose intensity decreases with increasing Al content. Panels (i, k, m) show [110]α diffraction patterns with superlattice reflections confirming DO₃ ordering (alongside B2) in ferrite, while (j, l, n) are corresponding TEM dark-field images indicating DO₃ ordered domain sizes of approximately 2.0 nm, 2.1 nm, and 19 nm in 6Al, 8Al, and 10Al steels respectively. Panel (o) captures acicular 18R martensite within 10Al ferrite, and (p) presents EDS elemental maps demonstrating Mn and C enrichment in these precipitates. Collectively, the data establish that increasing Al promotes ferrite formation, DO₃ domain coarsening, and brittle 18R martensite precipitation, underpinning the composition-dependent mechanical behavior.

Figure 2 presents XRD profiles and microhardness measurements for Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x = 4, 6, 8, 10 wt%) low-density steels. Panel (a) displays XRD patterns showing that increasing Al content drives a systematic phase transition from single-phase austenite (γ, FCC) in 4Al (100% γ), to austenite-dominated duplex in 6Al (97.0% γ, 3.0% α), ferrite-dominated duplex in 8Al (75.7% α, 24.3% γ), and nearly fully ferrite (α, BCC) in 10Al (98.1% α, 1.9% γ), with key diffraction peaks indexed as (111)γ, (200)γ, (220)γ, (110)α, and (211)α. Panel (b) plots the microhardness of individual γ and α phases versus Al content. Ferrite is consistently much harder than austenite and exhibits a steep increase from approximately 255 HV at 6Al to roughly 330 HV at 10Al, whereas austenite hardness increases more gradually from about 150 HV in 4Al to about 210 HV in 10Al. These results establish that Al strongly stabilizes BCC ferrite and that ferrite acts as the primary strengthening phase in the duplex microstructure, providing a critical foundation for tailoring strength-ductility combinations in these steels.

This figure presents the mechanical properties of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x = 4, 6, 8, 10 wt%) low-density steels. Subfigure (a) shows engineering stress-strain curves, where the 8Al steel achieves the highest ultimate tensile strength (~740 MPa) with good ductility, whereas the 10Al steel fractures prematurely at low strain (~4.6% elongation), indicating brittle behavior. Subfigure (b) summarizes yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS), uniform elongation (UEL), and total elongation (TEL) as functions of Al content, revealing that YS monotonically increases with Al, while UTS peaks at 8Al and ductility drops sharply at 10Al. Subfigure (c) displays true stress-strain curves confirming the superior strength-ductility balance of 8Al. Subfigure (d) presents strain hardening rate versus true strain curves: 8Al exhibits a distinctive five-stage hardening behavior (stages A–E) attributed to dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite, while 10Al shows rapid hardening rate decay. Subfigure (e) tracks microhardness evolution with strain, showing that 8Al ferrite initially softens then hardens, whereas austenite hardness increases monotonically, indicating divergent deformation mechanisms between the two phases. Collectively, these results demonstrate that the ferrite-based duplex 8Al steel achieves optimal mechanical performance through heterogeneous deformation coordination between ferrite and austenite, while the 10Al steel suffers from embrittlement due to coarsened DO3 ordered domains and 18R martensite formation.

This figure presents TEM bright-field micrographs with selected-area electron diffraction patterns (SAED) showing tensile-deformation substructures in Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C (x = 4, 6, 8, 10 wt.%) low-density steels. Subfigures (a, b, d, e, g) capture austenite (γ) morphology: (a) 4Al and (b) 6Al display dislocation cell blocks; (d) 8Al shows similar cell structures; (e) 8Al reveals sparse deformation twins associated with stress localization; and (g) 10Al exhibits complex planar-slip traces in early-deformation austenite. Subfigures (c, f, h, i) show ferrite (α) dislocation structures: (c) 6Al and (f) 8Al demonstrate pronounced planar-slip bands (rare in BCC), with 8Al showing stronger planar-slip character; whereas (h) and (i) 10Al ferrite contain only a few long straight screw dislocations and mixed dislocations, indicating brittle fracture. The systematic comparison evidences that increasing Al promotes planar slip in ferrite, but excessive Al (10Al) restricts dislocation activation to scarce screw dislocations, severely impairing ductility.

Figure 5 presents TEM bright-field images with selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns illustrating the strain-dependent microstructural evolution in austenite (γ) and ferrite (α) of Fe-35Mn-8Al-0.1C (8Al) duplex steel. At 3 % strain, austenite (a) shows planar dislocation slip on {111} planes with cross-slip, while ferrite (b,c) exhibits slip bands composed of edge, mixed, and screw dislocations on {112} planes. At 10 %, cross-slip intensifies in austenite (d) and slip bands evolve in ferrite (e). At 20 %, austenite forms dislocation cell blocks (f) and dislocation cells (g), whereas ferrite slip bands refine (h). At 28 %, austenite displays further refined cell blocks (i), cells (j), and deformation twins (k), while ferrite (l) shows refined slip bands with cross-slip. SAED insets confirm the γ [110] and [100] zone axes and α [110] and [011] axes, with DO3 superlattice reflections visible in ferrite patterns. These observations support dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite and progressive dislocation substructure formation in austenite as key deformation mechanisms.

This figure presents a line chart quantifying the strain-dependent refinement of dislocation substructures in the 8Al steel (Fe-35Mn-8Al-0.1C), a ferrite-austenite duplex low-density steel, under tensile deformation. The x-axis shows engineering strain from 0 to 0.28, while the y-axis gives size in nanometers. Three datasets with error bars are plotted: α_SB (black) denotes the average slip band spacing in ferrite; γ_SB+DCB (red) denotes the average slip band plus dislocation cell block size in austenite; and γ_DC (blue) denotes the dislocation cell size in austenite. All three parameters decrease monotonically with increasing strain, indicating progressive microstructure refinement. The ferrite slip band spacing falls from approximately 780 nm to about 80 nm, while austenite cell structures evolve from slip bands into finer dislocation cell blocks and cells. Caption states these are statistically analyzed averages at different deformation stages. These measured D values are subsequently used as microstructural inputs in the composite flow stress model (Eqs. 5–8) to predict the overall stress-strain response, thereby supporting the dynamic slip band refinement mechanism and the synergistic strengthening between ferrite and austenite.

This figure presents twelve TEM two-beam bright-field micrographs illustrating the dislocation morphologies in ferrite (α) of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels after 3% tensile engineering strain. Subfigures (a)–(f) correspond to 10Al steel and (g)–(l) to 8Al steel, each acquired near the [111] zone axis with different g diffraction vectors (e.g., g = [10 1̄], [1̄ 1̄ 0], [0 1̄ 1̄]) as shown in the SAED insets. Through g·b = 0 analysis, the Burgers vector of dislocations in both steels is determined to be a/2[111]. In 10Al steel, long straight screw dislocations predominate with a non-uniform distribution. In contrast, 8Al steel exhibits a heterogeneous activation of edge, mixed, and screw dislocations, where edge dislocations are readily activated at ferrite grain boundaries and ferrite-austenite (α/γ) interfaces (j–l). The approximately 24.3% austenite phase in 8Al steel acts as a soft phase to alleviate stress localization. These observations reveal that the inferior ductility of 10Al steel stems from limited screw dislocation activity, whereas the superior strength-ductility balance of 8Al steel is attributed to multiple dislocation types and phase-boundary coordinated deformation.

This figure presents a comprehensive TEM investigation of planar slip and dislocation configurations in the ferrite phase of Fe-35Mn-xAl-0.1C low-density steels, comparing 10Al (a–c) and 8Al (d–i) samples. Subfigures (a) and (d) show bright-field images of slip bands with SAED patterns along [100], where red and blue arrows mark dislocation pairs. Enlarged views in (b) and (e) reveal that 10Al ferrite contains predominantly paired screw dislocations, whereas 8Al ferrite exhibits screw, edge, and mixed dislocations with less pronounced pairing. Dark-field images (c, f) using the (1-1-1) DO3 superlattice reflection demonstrate that nanoscale DO3 ordered domains in 10Al cannot be completely sheared by dislocations, while those in 8Al are readily sheared. High-resolution TEM (g) and corresponding IFFT images (h, i) along the (011) and (0-11) planes resolve that dislocations comprise numerous dislocation dipoles shearing the DO3 superlattice along both the dislocation line and slip plane. These observations support that larger DO3 domains and higher APB energy in 10Al impede complete shearing, causing pronounced dislocation pairing and brittle behavior, whereas smaller domains in 8Al permit extensive planar slip and superior ductility.

Figure 9 presents the relationship between deformation microstructure and flow stress in Fe35MnxAl0.1C low-density steels. Panel (a) plots the normalized shear stress (τ-τ0)/G/10^-3 versus the normalized inverse spacing b/D/10^-3 for the austenite phase in 4Al steel, yielding similitude constants K_DC = 2.02 (dislocation cells, gray squares and black line) and K_SB+DCB = 2.03 (slip bands plus dislocation cell blocks, red circles and red line). These K values fall close to the reported range of 2.0–2.9 for austenitic stainless steels, and because the austenite phases in 4Al and 8Al steels share similar compositional systems and deformation structures, the constants obtained from 4Al are used to approximate the austenite behavior in the duplex 8Al steel. Panel (b) compares the measured true stress (black squares and line) with the calculated total flow stress σ_0+σ_α(ε)+σ_γ(ε) (red squares and line) for 8Al steel at true strains of approximately 0.025, 0.10, 0.175, and 0.25. The stacked columns decompose the calculated stress into the base stress σ_0 (~455 MPa, orange), the ferrite contribution σ_α(ε) (green), and the austenite contribution σ_γ(ε) (purple). Both phase contributions increase with strain, with σ_α dominating the strain hardening, and the close agreement between calculated and measured stresses validates the composite flow stress model based on dynamic slip band refinement in ferrite and dislocation cell evolution in austenite.
